course_info.title: Data Structures and Algorithms
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Formally analyze algorithm time and space complexity
Apply Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta notation correctly
Analyze loops, nested loops, and recursive algorithms
readings: CLRS, Ch. 2-3 - Getting Started and Growth of Functions
Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 1.4 - Analysis of Algorithms

lecture_notes.introduction: Data Structures and Algorithms is the most important course in the CS curriculum. Every technical interview, every system design decision, and every performance optimization depends on the concepts you learn here.
key_concepts: Worst-case, average-case, and best-case analysis
Big-O (upper bound), Big-Omega (lower bound), Big-Theta (tight bound)
Analyzing iterative algorithms: counting operations in loops
Analyzing recursive algorithms: recurrence relations
Amortized analysis: average cost over a sequence of operations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze the time complexity of five code snippets ranging from O(1) to O(n^3), showing how to count operations systematically
setup_requirements: IDE configured for the chosen language

review.value: Big-O is an upper bound (at most this fast); Big-Theta is a tight bound (exactly this fast asymptotically). Saying binary search is O(n) is technically true but imprecise; O(log n) and Theta(log n) are the correct characterizations
Some operations are expensive rarely but cheap usually; amortized analysis averages cost over a sequence. ArrayList append is O(1) amortized even though individual resizes are O(n)

learning_objectives: Implement stack and queue ADTs using arrays and linked lists
Understand trade-offs between array-based and pointer-based implementations
Apply stacks and queues to solve practical problems
readings: Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 1.3 - Bags, Queues, and Stacks

lecture_notes.introduction: Stacks and queues are the simplest abstract data types that restrict how elements are added and removed. Despite their simplicity, they are fundamental building blocks: function calls use a stack, BFS uses a queue, and undo systems use a stack.
key_concepts: Stack ADT: push, pop, peek — LIFO ordering
Queue ADT: enqueue, dequeue, front — FIFO ordering
Singly and doubly linked list implementation
Array-based vs linked implementation trade-offs
Applications: expression evaluation, BFS, undo systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a balanced parentheses checker using a stack and a print job scheduler using a queue
setup_requirements: Understanding of Big-O from previous session

review.value: Arrays give O(1) random access but O(n) insertion/deletion in the middle; linked lists give O(1) insertion/deletion at known positions but O(n) access by index
Function call stack, expression parsing, undo/redo, DFS traversal, backtracking algorithms — all use LIFO ordering

learning_objectives: Implement a binary search tree with insert, search, and delete
Perform in-order, pre-order, and post-order traversals
readings: Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 3.2 - Binary Search Trees

lecture_notes.introduction: Trees are hierarchical data structures that appear everywhere: file systems, HTML DOM, database indexes, decision trees, and compiler parse trees. Binary search trees combine the flexibility of linked lists with the fast lookup of binary search.
key_concepts: Tree terminology: root, parent, child, leaf, height, depth
Binary tree properties and the BST invariant
BST operations: insert, search, find min/max — all O(h)
BST delete: three cases (leaf, one child, two children)
Tree traversals: in-order (sorted), pre-order, post-order, level-order
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a BST from a sequence of insertions, perform all traversal types, and walk through the three delete cases visually

review.value: For every node, all keys in the left subtree are less than the node's key, and all keys in the right subtree are greater; this invariant enables O(h) search
If elements are inserted in sorted order, the BST degenerates to a linked list with O(n) operations; balanced trees (AVL, Red-Black) prevent this

learning_objectives: Understand hash function design and properties
Implement a hash table with chaining and open addressing
Analyze average and worst-case performance of hash tables

lecture_notes.introduction: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case lookup, insertion, and deletion — the fastest possible for key-value storage. Understanding how they work under the hood explains why Python dictionaries and Java HashMaps are so fast.
key_concepts: Hash function requirements: deterministic, uniform distribution, fast computation
Collision resolution: separate chaining with linked lists
Collision resolution: open addressing (linear probing, quadratic probing, double hashing)
Load factor and dynamic resizing
Worst-case O(n) when all keys collide
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a hash table from scratch with chaining, insert 1000 random keys, and visualize the distribution of chain lengths to observe uniform hashing

review.value: The ratio of elements to buckets (n/m); when it exceeds a threshold (typically 0.75), the table is resized and all elements are rehashed, keeping average chain length constant
Chaining handles collisions with linked lists (simpler, no clustering); open addressing probes alternative slots (better cache performance, but degrades badly at high load factors)

class_schedule.topic: Sorting Algorithms: Merge Sort and Quicksort
learning_objectives: Implement merge sort and quicksort from scratch
Analyze their time complexity using recurrence relations
Understand when to use each algorithm and their stability properties
readings: CLRS, Ch. 7 - Quicksort and Ch. 2.3 - Merge Sort
Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 2.2-2.3 - Mergesort and Quicksort

lecture_notes.introduction: Merge sort and quicksort are the two most important comparison-based sorting algorithms. Merge sort guarantees O(n log n) worst-case; quicksort achieves O(n log n) average-case with better constants and is the basis for most standard library sort implementations.
key_concepts: Merge sort: divide, recursively sort, merge — O(n log n) worst-case, O(n) extra space
Quicksort: partition around pivot, recursively sort — O(n log n) average, O(n^2) worst-case
Pivot selection strategies: first, random, median-of-three
Stability: merge sort is stable, quicksort is not
Lower bound: comparison-based sorting cannot be faster than O(n log n)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement both algorithms, then benchmark them on random, sorted, and reverse-sorted arrays of varying sizes to observe their behavior empirically

review.value: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n); by the Master Theorem, this solves to T(n) = O(n log n). The O(n) term is the merge step

Occurs when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (sorted input with first-element pivot), causing T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) = O(n^2); randomized pivot selection makes this astronomically unlikely

learning_objectives: Represent graphs using adjacency lists and adjacency matrices
Implement breadth-first search and depth-first search
Apply graph traversal to solve reachability and shortest path problems

lecture_notes.introduction: Graphs model relationships: social networks, road maps, dependency chains, web links, circuit boards. BFS and DFS are the foundation of nearly every graph algorithm, from shortest paths to cycle detection to topological sorting.
key_concepts: Graph terminology: vertex, edge, directed vs undirected, weighted vs unweighted
Adjacency list vs adjacency matrix: space and time trade-offs
BFS: level-order exploration using a queue — O(V + E)
DFS: depth-first exploration using recursion/stack — O(V + E)
Applications: shortest path (unweighted), cycle detection, connected components, topological sort
lecture_notes.demonstration: Model a campus map as a graph, implement BFS to find the shortest path between buildings, and DFS to detect if all buildings are reachable from the main entrance

review.value: Adjacency list uses O(V + E) space and is efficient for sparse graphs; adjacency matrix uses O(V^2) space but allows O(1) edge lookup. Most real-world graphs are sparse

BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs and explores level by level; DFS explores as deep as possible first and is the basis for topological sort, cycle detection, and strongly connected components

quizzes.topic: Complexity Analysis and Linear Data Structures

questions.answer: O(n log n). The outer while loop runs log(n) times because i doubles each iteration. The inner for loop runs n times for each outer iteration. Total: n * log(n).

rubric.key: Correctly identifies outer loop as O(log n)
Correctly identifies inner loop as O(n)
Correctly multiplies to O(n log n)

questions.question: Describe one advantage and one disadvantage of using a linked list instead of a dynamic array for implementing a stack.
questions.answer: Advantage: Linked list push/pop is always O(1) with no amortized cost, since there is no resizing. Disadvantage: Each element requires extra memory for the pointer/reference to the next node, and linked list nodes are scattered in memory, leading to poor cache performance.

questions.question:
Given the following BST, what is the in-order traversal output?

        15
       /  \
      10   20
     / \     \
    5   12   25
         \
         14
questions.answer: 5, 10, 12, 14, 15, 20, 25. In-order traversal visits left subtree, then root, then right subtree, producing elements in sorted order for a BST.

rubric.key: Explains in-order produces sorted output for BST

questions.question: Explain why quicksort is generally faster than merge sort in practice despite having a worse worst-case complexity.
questions.answer: Quicksort has better cache locality because it sorts in-place (elements are close in memory), while merge sort requires O(n) auxiliary space for merging. Quicksort's inner loop does fewer comparisons per element (smaller constant factor). The O(n^2) worst case is avoided in practice with randomized pivot selection. Additionally, quicksort's in-place nature means less memory allocation overhead.

rubric.key: Mentions smaller constant factor or fewer operations per element
Notes that worst case is avoidable with randomized pivot
Mentions memory allocation overhead of merge sort

major_assignments.title: Autocomplete Engine
major_assignments.description: Build an autocomplete engine that efficiently suggests completions as a user types. Implement and compare a trie-based solution against a sorted-array binary search solution, benchmarking both with a dictionary of 100,000+ words.
objectives: Implement a trie data structure with insert, search, and prefix-match operations
Implement a binary search approach for prefix matching on sorted arrays
Benchmark and compare both approaches with empirical timing data
Analyze the time and space complexity of each approach
deliverables: Source code for trie and binary search implementations
Benchmark results comparing both approaches on varying input sizes
Analysis report (2-3 pages) with complexity analysis and empirical results
pytest test suite covering correctness and edge cases

criteria: Trie insert, search, and prefix-match are correct
Binary search prefix-match is correct
Both handle edge cases (empty input, no matches, single character)
Code is clean and well-organized
Correct Big-O analysis for both approaches
Space complexity comparison is accurate
Empirical benchmarks support the theoretical analysis
Trade-offs are clearly articulated
Comprehensive test suite covering normal and edge cases
Tests verify correctness of both implementations
Benchmark methodology is sound and reproducible
All tests pass
Analysis report is clear and well-structured
Benchmark methodology is explained
Code has appropriate comments and docstrings
README explains how to run benchmarks
Correct trie and binary search implementations with prefix matching
Rigorous complexity analysis supported by empirical benchmarks
Comprehensive test suite and clean code organization
Clear analysis report comparing approaches

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of data structure and algorithm concepts

lab_assignments.description: Weekly implementation exercises for each data structure and algorithm

major_assignments.description: Larger projects requiring design, implementation, and analysis

midterm_examination.description: Written exam covering data structures and sorting algorithms

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all course material including graphs

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late. Extensions require advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of algorithms and approaches is encouraged. All code and written analysis must be individual work. Sharing implementation code constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Using solutions from the internet, other students, or AI tools without attribution is plagiarism and will result in a zero on the assignment.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop with Python 3.12+ or Java 17+, an IDE, and Git. Benchmarking tools will be introduced in class.
